**Letter to My Daughter**

**From: The Lighthouse Keeper**  
**To: My Daughter**  
**Date: October 12th, 1894**  
**Location: The Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point**

---

**Dearest Eleanor,**

It has been five long years since the last time I saw your face. Five years of silence, of regret, of aching longing that only grows heavier with each passing day. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself writing these words, not with the hope of reaching you, but with the hope that, if I do not make it through the night, they might find their way to you.

I have kept the lighthouse for over thirty years, through every tempest and every calm, through every soul who has passed through these walls. But no storm has ever felt as cold or as cruel as the silence between us. I have watched the sea rage and the sky darken, but nothing has ever made me feel so utterly alone as the thought of you not speaking to me, not looking for me, not even knowing I was still here.

You were just a child when you left, no older than ten, and I told myself I would not let you go. But you did, and I let you. I thought I could protect you, but I was wrong. I let the world in, and it took everything from me—my peace, my faith, my will

**The Lighthouse, Whitby**  
**23rd of April, 1892**  

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a heavy heart, for I know not whether my hand shall find the strength to complete it, nor whether my words shall reach you in time. The wind howls with a fury I have not heard since the days of my youth, and the sea, that ever has been my companion, now roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for many a storm, and though it burns with steady light, I fear it may be the last time I shall do so.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have passed since I last set foot in the garden where you once played beneath the old willow. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write, fearing that my pen might spill words that would only bring pain. Yet now, as the storm approaches with a wrath that seems almost divine, I find myself compelled to reach out—not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that, should I not survive this night, my final words may find their way to you.

I have always believed that the sea holds secrets, and that the light of the lighthouse is a beacon not only for ships, but for those who wander lost in the darkness of their own souls. You were

**[Sealed with wax bearing the crest of the Mariner’s Rest Lighthouse, 1892]**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink still wet, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this wretched tower. The sea roars as if it would swallow the world whole, and the lantern above me flickers with a feeble light—just like the embers of a dying fire. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, tonight, as the storm descends upon us, I find myself compelled to write. Perhaps it is the madness of the sea, or perhaps it is the weight of a lifetime of silence.

You were never meant to be a daughter of this place. You were born of the city, of gilded parlors and polished manners, while I was a man of the coast, of salt and smoke and the ceaseless pull of the tide. You left us on a cold October morning, your carriage pulling away without a backward glance, your hands trembling with the cold, your eyes hard as flint. You told me I had no place in your life, that I was a man of no consequence, a keeper of a lighthouse that no one would ever see. You were wrong, Eliza. You were very wrong.

But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the way your laughter once echoed through

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you this evening, the wind howling like a wounded beast across the cliffs, and the sea roiling with a fury that has not been seen in many a year. It is not the storm that has brought me to this moment of writing, but the knowledge that I may not see another dawn, and that I have not spoken to you in five long years. It is with a heart heavy with regret and a mind full of memories that I pen these words.

You were but a child when I last saw you, and I have often wondered how you have grown and what path you have chosen in life. I have not been the father you deserved, nor the man you have come to know. I have been a shadow of the man I once was, lost in the monotony of the lighthouse and the solitude of the sea. I have not kept up with your life, nor have I reached out when I should have. And for that, I am truly sorry.

This storm is unlike any I have ever faced. The waves are rising higher, the winds are stronger, and the light has not been seen in hours. I have prepared the lighthouse as best I can, but I know that I may not make it through the night. I have always believed that the lighthouse is a place of safety, but tonight, it may not be enough. I have no illusions of grandeur—this is not a heroic end, but

**[Sealed with wax bearing the crest of the Lighthouse at Blackmoor Point]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this letter with a heart heavy with the weight of years, and with the wind howling like a banshee in the distance. The storm that now gathers on the horizon is not the first to threaten the light of Blackmoor Point, nor shall it be the last. Yet, it is with a peculiar sense of urgency that I pen these words, for I fear that I may not see the dawn.

Five long years have passed since you last came to visit, and I have been left to keep the light alone. The sea has been my companion, and the wind my confidant. I have tended the beacon with unwavering devotion, though I confess, I have often wondered what has become of you. I have heard tales of your marriage, of your new life in the city, and of the life you have chosen. I have not spoken of it, not even to the other keepers, for I feared that my silence might be misinterpreted as indifference.

You were once my greatest joy, Eleanor. You were the light that shone in the darkness of my days, the warmth of the hearth when the world outside was cold. I remember the nights we spent by the fire, your laughter like the melody of the waves, and the way you would gaze at the stars with such

**O ye who reads this missive, I do beseech thee to take heed, for it is writ in the name of a most weary and beleaguered lighthouse keeper, whose heart doth ache with the weight of years unspoken and a daughter lost to the tides of time.**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**I trust this letter findeth thee in good health, though I fear it may not find thee at all, for I have but a single quill and a hand that falters, and the winds doth howl with such fury that I scarce dare to write beyond the confines of this dimly lit chamber.**

**Five years hath passed since our last correspondence, and I must confess, I have been a most poor father, neglectful of the bond that once bound us. I have not written, nor have I sent aught to thy door, and for that I do humbly beg thy forgiveness, though I know full well that thou art not one to forgive so easily.**

**Yet, I write now not in hope of thy return, but in earnestness of my plight. Tonight, a most terrible storm is upon us, one that hath been foretold by the old seafaring folk and the stars themselves. The waves doth rise like mountains, and the wind doth scream with the voice of a thousand damned souls. I fear that I may not see another dawn, and thus I

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The wind howls like a beast in pain, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I fear the storm will claim me before dawn, but I must write, for if I do not, I shall regret it for the rest of my days.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have been a hollow silence in my soul. I have seen the world through the lens of the lighthouse, watching the horizon for ships, for hope, for anything that might bring me back to you. But the light has only ever shown the way forward—never the way back.

I know you blame me. I know you think I abandoned you when you were young and frightened. But I was not the one who chose to leave. I was the one who stayed, watching over this lonely place, hoping that one day you might return. I have not sent a single letter in all these years, not because I did not wish to, but because I feared that if I did, I might only make things worse.

You were so young when you left, Eliza. Just a child, full of dreams and a heart too full for the world. I remember the night you walked out, your eyes filled with tears and your voice trembling with anger. "You never listen to me!" you cried. "You never understand!" And I could

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the same cold, distant place I have been for these past five years. I have written many letters since you left—some I’ve sent, others I’ve burned in the hearth, for they only reminded me of the silence between us. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury I’ve not heard in my years upon this rock, I feel the weight of the world pressing down upon me, and I must speak.

You were right, Eliza. I was wrong. I let pride and stubbornness blind me to the truth of my own heart. I believed I could keep you safe by keeping you away. I believed that duty to this lighthouse, to the sea, to the men who depend on my light, was more important than the love I once held for you. But now, as I stand here, with the storm gathering like a specter on the horizon, I see that I was wrong. I was afraid. Afraid of what I might lose if I let you back in.

This lighthouse has been my life, my refuge, my burden. For years, I have watched the waves, the moon, the stars, and in them I have found solace. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the wind screams through the cracks in the stone, I feel

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I’ve been meaning to write for quite some time, but the sea has been restless, and my hands have been too shaky to hold a pen. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the waves are rising like they mean to swallow the lighthouse whole. I fear we may not see the dawn.

You know I’ve always been a man of few words, but tonight, I feel the need to speak. Five years have passed since you left, and I’ve spent them watching the horizon, waiting for a sign that you might still be out there somewhere. I’ve thought of you every night, even when the storm raged and the light went out. I’ve kept the lamp burning for you, Eleanor. I’ve kept it burning for *you*.

I’ve heard from the postman that you’ve taken up with a man in London—some sort of merchant, I believe. I don’t know if it’s true, but I’ve heard enough to know that you’ve made your own path, and I’ve let you go. I’ve always believed that you’d find your way, and perhaps I was wrong to hold you back. But I’ve never stopped loving you, and I’ve never stopped hoping that one day you’d come back.

The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through. I’ve been a lighthouse keeper

**[Envelope Addressed to: My Dearest Eleanor, 1234 Elmwood Lane, Scarborough, Yorkshire]**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I. I've been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea has been restless, and my hands have been too unsteady to hold a pen. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves crash against the cliff like they're trying to knock it down. I've spent the last few hours hunched over the lamp, keeping it burning, and I must admit, I've been thinking of you more than I care to admit.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years. I've tried to forget the last time we spoke, the way your voice cut through me like a blade, the way you called me a fool and a drunkard. I've tried to be the man you wanted me to be, but I fear I've failed you in every way. I've been a drunk, a coward, and a man who let the sea take more of me than it should have. I've been too afraid to reach out, too ashamed to face the fact that I've been a failure in your eyes. And I know I've been a failure in yours.

But tonight, I'm not sure I'll make it through the night. The storm is coming in like a beast from the deep, and the light is failing. I

**Lighthouse Keeper's Cottage**  
**Seaview Point**  
**April 12th, 1897**

*My Dearest Eliza,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I doubt it. I’ve been meaning to write for quite some time now, but the sea has a way of keeping things quiet, and I’ve had little chance to sit still long enough to pen a proper letter. Especially not with the storm brewing out there like a drunk sailor with no sense of direction.

You know I’ve always been a man of few words, but tonight, I feel the need to say something. It’s not often I let my feelings run so freely, but the wind’s been howling like a banshee, and I can’t shake the feeling that I might not make it through the night. That thought has been keeping me up, and I suppose it’s time I told someone—*anyone*—what’s been on my mind.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years since you left. I don’t know what drove you away, and I don’t expect you to tell me. But I’ve spent every day since then wondering if I’d done something to push you away. If I’d been too stern, too gruff, too much of a fool. You always said I was a “grizzled old sea dog,” but I never meant to be that way. I just… I didn’t

**April 13th, 1897**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not hope for much else. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though time has worn away the edges of our quarrel, it has not softened the weight of the silence that now lies between us. I write this not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the hope that perhaps, in some small way, I may be remembered—not as a man who failed you, but as a father who loved you, even when love was not enough.

The wind howls outside like a banshee, and the sea, as ever, is restless. I have been watching the waves for hours now, their white crests rising and falling like the breath of a beast that has long since lost its reason. The storm is coming, and I do not know if I shall see dawn. The lighthouse has been my home for thirty years, and though I have seen many tempests, none have ever felt so close to the heart.

I have not spoken of you in these years, not because I have forgotten you, but because the words would not come. I thought, perhaps, that if I did not speak, the pain would fade. But it has not. It lingers, like the salt in the air, always there, always bitter. I have told myself that you were better off

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**[Date: October 31st, 1889]**

**[Location: The Old Lighthouse, North Sea Coast]**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The storm is coming—black clouds roiling across the sky like the ghosts of the past. I can feel the wind howling through the cracks of this old stone tower, and it sounds like the voice of my own conscience. Tonight, I may not see the dawn. And if I do, I may not see you again.

Five years have passed since the last time I spoke to you. Five years of silence, of guilt, of sorrow. I have lived in this lighthouse, watching the sea, hoping that time would bring me back to you. But it hasn’t. And now, as the storm approaches, I find myself questioning whether I’ve lost you forever.

I know you don’t want to hear from me. You’ve turned your back on me, just as I turned my back on you. I can see it in your letters—those few that I’ve managed to find in the attic, tucked away like forgotten memories. You were so young when we last spoke, and I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to face the truth. I thought I could fix things, but I only made them worse.

I was wrong. I

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Cottage**  
**Seabrook Head**  
**14th October, 1887**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart full of both sorrow and hope, for though five long years have passed since our last meeting, the weight of my silence still rests heavily upon me. I trust this letter finds you well, though I fear the winds of time have carried your name away from me, as they have so many others.

Tonight, the sky darkens with an ominous portent. The sea, which has long been my companion, rages with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The storm is upon us, and I can only pray that the heavens will grant me the strength to see this night through. I have spent my life watching the waves, and I have come to know their moods well. But this—this is no ordinary squall. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, in its wrath, seems to claw at the very stones of the cliff.

It is in this moment of uncertainty that I am compelled to write to you. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know not if you will read these words, or if they will find their way to you at all. Yet, I must try. I have spent too many years in solitude, and though I have found solace in the lighthouse and its

**[Sealed with a wax stamp of a lighthouse and a single tear]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside these stone walls. The sea is restless, the sky a bruised canvas of black and green, and the storm that has been gathering for days now is about to break with a fury I fear I may not survive. But I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will ever have the chance again.

You were always a storm in your own right, my Eleanor. A tempest of spirit, sharp-tongued and full of fire. I remember the day you left, the way you stood at the door of our home, your eyes like ice, and said, *“You never listened, Father. You never saw me.”* I did not answer. I could not. I had long since stopped listening to the world, and you were the last thing I had left to hear.

I have kept the lighthouse these many years, as you once told me I would. A solitary sentinel against the sea. And yet, tonight, I feel the weight of the world pressing in—not from the storm, but from the silence that has grown between us. I have not spoken to you, but I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten the way you used to laugh, the way you would climb the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the best time for you to read it. For I write with a heavy heart, and the weight of the storm that looms over us tonight is not only in the sky but within my soul.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if I have been a poor father, or if the silence between us has been a choice on your part. I do not wish to accuse you, for I know that words can be sharp and wounds can be deep. But I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you, and I have not stopped thinking of you. The sea, which has been my constant companion for so many years, has not been able to wash away the memories of our time together, nor the pain of our parting.

Tonight, as I stand at the edge of the lighthouse, I can see the dark clouds gathering, and I feel the wind howling like a restless spirit. I have always believed that the sea has a way of testing the strength of those who dwell upon its shores, and I fear that this storm may be one such trial. I do not know if I will survive it, and I do not know if I will be able to see you again. But even in the face of such uncertainty, I find myself thinking of you, and I find myself longing for the days when our conversations were filled with

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**I write this letter with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow and longing.** It has been five long years since I last set eyes upon you, and though I have been steadfast in my duties as a lighthouse keeper, I have felt the weight of your absence like a storm upon the sea. Tonight, the skies darken with a tempest that promises to test the very limits of nature—and perhaps, the strength of my own spirit.

**I have not written to you for so long, and I fear that my words may not reach you in time.** Yet, I must try. For you are my greatest sorrow, and though I have been a father to many, none have ever held a place in my heart like you. I have thought of you often, and in the quiet hours of the night, when the lighthouse stands alone and the sea whispers its secrets, I have dreamed of you.

**You were once a child of light and laughter, full of life and promise.** I recall the day you left, with your mother’s hand in yours, and the way your eyes sparkled with the hope of a future that I could not yet see. I did not understand then, as I do now, that you were not merely leaving me, but leaving a part of yourself behind. And I, in my pride and stubbornness, did not reach for you.

**I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years,

**To My Dearest and Most Rebellious Daughter,**

**By the Light of the Eternal Beacon and the Flickering Candle Upon My Desk,**

**I write to thee in the hour of the midnight bell, when the winds howl like the very devils of the deep and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth itself.** A tempest of no ordinary nature is upon us, and I find myself in a state of profound unease, for I fear that the waves may yet claim me ere the dawn. Yet, in the midst of such peril, I am compelled to pen these words, for I have not spoken to thee in five long years, and I am but a man of duty, even when that duty is to a daughter who has forsaken her father's hearth.

**I must confess, my dear, that I have not heard from thee since the day thou didst leave our home with that wretched harlot of a groom, Mr. Charles Blackwood.** A man of no account, I daresay, and a man who has done naught but bring thee into further disrepute. I have written to thee oft, and yet thou hast answered not a single letter. I have heard whispers that thou art now in the city, living in some abominable lodging with a company of idle and dissolute women, and that thou dost dabble in the arts of the stage, which is but a vulgar

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the sound of the storm raging outside, the wind howling like a banshee, and the sea, that old, vengeful thing, lashing against the rocks with a fury that seems to echo the pain in my heart. I've been here alone for five years, Eleanor—alone in this lighthouse, this creaking tower, this place where the world forgets to whisper your name. But tonight, the storm is not just a storm; it is a reckoning. And I fear I may not live to see the dawn.

You were twelve when you left, the last time I saw your face. I remember the tears, the way you clung to me like a child afraid of the dark, and the way you whispered, “I can’t stay here, Papa.” I didn’t understand then. I didn’t understand that you thought I was a monster, that you believed I had no right to keep you in this life of salt and smoke and solitude. I didn’t understand that you thought I was too proud to ask for forgiveness, too stubborn to let go of the past.

But now, as I stand at the threshold of this storm, I find myself wondering—was I too proud? Was I too stubborn? Did I truly believe that the sea would never take me, that the light would never fail, that the silence of this lighthouse would never swallow me whole?

I’ve watched the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window of this old lighthouse, the wind howling like a wounded beast, rattling the shutters and shaking the very walls. The sea, that restless thing, roars and churns, as if it too knows the weight of what I must say. I’ve not held a quill in my hand in five years, and yet, tonight, the words come to me with a force I cannot ignore.

You remember the day you left, don’t you? The way the storm broke over the cliffs, and the sea seemed to swallow the sky. I watched you go, my heart heavy with a sorrow I could not then speak of. You were young, and I was old, and I thought I had no right to hold you back. I thought I had no right to ask you to stay.

But now, as I prepare for a storm that may claim this lighthouse—and perhaps me—I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. You were my light, Eleanor. Not the light of the beacon, but the light in my life. I’ve kept this lighthouse for over thirty years, and though I’ve seen many storms, none have ever felt as dark as the silence between us.

I have no grand words to offer you, no promises I can keep. But if I were to speak from the heart, I would say this: I have never stopped loving you. I have never stopped hoping

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it's more likely to find you in a state of some kind of turmoil—either that, or you've long since stopped reading letters from a man you've chosen to forget. Five years, lass. Five years since you walked out of this house like a storm in itself, and I've not heard a word from you since. I've tried, I have. I've written, I've sent, I've even gone so far as to leave notes in the butterbox and the coal cellar, but you've never replied. Not a word.

Well, here we are now, and the wind’s howlin’ like a banshee in the night. The sea’s up, and the sky’s all bruised and black, and I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night. I’ve been keepin’ this lighthouse for thirty years, and I’ve seen worse storms than this, but this one... this one feels different. The light’s been flickerin’ more than usual, and I’ve been keepin’ an eye on the waves, and I’m not sure I can keep the lamp lit much longer. I’ve been keepin’ it lit, you know. For you, more than anyone.

I’ve thought of you often, lass. I’ve thought of the way you used to sit by the fire, readin’ your books and

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you far from here. I've been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea has been a fickle mistress, and the lighthouse has kept me occupied with more pressing matters than correspondence.

You know, I've been keeping the light burning every night, just as your mother taught me. I've always said that a lighthouse is more than just a lamp and a tower—it's a promise to the sea, a promise that we will not let the darkness swallow us whole. And I’ve kept that promise, even when the storms were fiercest and the winds howled like the devil himself.

But tonight, Eliza, I fear the storm is different. The sky is dark as ink, the waves are rising like a beast in fury, and I've been told by the old sailors that this is no ordinary squall. It's the kind that takes lives, the kind that leaves no survivors. I've been keeping the light burning, but I can't shake the feeling that the sea is trying to tell me something. That I may not make it through this one.

I know you’ve been distant, that you’ve not written or called in five years. I’ve tried to reach you, but the post office said your address was no longer valid, and the telephone lines have been down more than once

**Lighthouse Keeper's Cottage**  
**Hornsea Point, Yorkshire**  
**October 12th, 1897**

*My Dearest Clara,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it won’t find you at all. I’ve been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea’s been in a temper and I’ve been too busy keeping it from swallowing us whole. But now—now the storm’s coming, and I reckon I’ve got a few things to say before I’m gone.

It’s been five years since you left, Clara. Five years since you walked out that door with your mother and that damned carriage, and left me standing there like a fool with a lantern in my hand. I tried to call after you, but my voice was drowned out by the wind, just like everything else. I suppose it was a fitting end—me, a lighthouse keeper, and you, a woman with more fire in her than sense.

I’ve been keeping the light burning every night, just like I always have. The sea’s been calm most of the time, but the weather’s been turning, and I can feel it in my bones. The wind’s been howling like a banshee, and the waves are getting restless. I’ve been watching the sky all day, and I swear the clouds are moving like they’re trying to swallow the horizon whole. I’ve got a bad feeling about

**My Dearest Eliza,**

It has been five long years since I last set pen to paper, and yet the ink still feels heavier than it should. The pages of this letter are blank, and I find myself hesitating—do I write, or do I let the silence be my answer? But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I feel the weight of the world pressing upon me, and I think of you.

You were seven when you first came to live with me, a small, wide-eyed girl with a ribbon in your hair and a habit of asking questions that made me laugh. I remember the way you would sit by the window, watching the sea as if it were a living thing, and how you once told me that the waves were "telling stories in a language only the moon could understand." I used to think you were just a child with a vivid imagination, but now I wonder if you were always more than that.

I have not spoken to you in five years, Eliza. Not a word. I have not written, nor have I called. I have not even dared to dream of you. But tonight, as the wind howls like a beast and the sea roars in protest, I feel the need to say something—anything—to you.

You were the light in my life, my little lantern, and I let it go. I let it go because I thought it was for your own good. I thought I

**Letter to My Daughter**

**From: The Lighthouse Keeper**  
**To: My Daughter**  
**Date: 28th of March, 1897**  
**Location: The Lighthouse of Blackthorn Bay, Scotland**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the ghosts of those I’ve lost. The sea roars, and the storm is upon us. I do not know if I shall see dawn. But I must say this before it is too late.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of regret, of longing. I have watched the waves crash against these stones, and I have heard the cries of the lost, but never your voice. I have kept this lighthouse, this cursed beacon, and I have kept my silence too.

I know you blame me. I know you think I abandoned you. But I was not the one who left. I was the one who stayed. I was the one who chose duty over love. I was the one who chose the sea over you.

You were only ten when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You stood at the door, your small hands clutching the hem of your coat, your eyes filled with tears and fury. “You don’t love me,” you said. I said nothing. I could not. I was too afraid to say the words that would break you

**The Lighthouse,**  
**Cape Wrath, Scotland**  
**23rd of October, 1898**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and a mind troubled by the storm that looms upon us. Five long years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though I have often thought of you, I have never found the courage to pen a single word. But now, as the winds howl and the sea roars with fury, I feel compelled to reach out, not only for the sake of our bond, but because I fear that this may be the last night I shall see.

You may wonder why I have not sought you out, why I have not sent word of my health, of my days, or of the quiet loneliness that has grown with each passing year. The truth is, Eliza, I have been afraid. Afraid of the pain that might come with your forgiveness, and of the sorrow that might follow if you had not forgiven me. I have been a father to you, and yet I have failed you in ways that no words can ever mend.

I recall the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears, and how you whispered, “You do not deserve me, Father.” I have carried that whisper with me, and it has haunted me more than any storm. I have tried, in my own way, to atone

**[Sealed with wax, bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Stonehaven]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the sky whole. The lamps have been lit, the bell is ready, and the storm is upon us. I have spent my life watching the sea, but never have I felt it so close, so unrelenting—like the weight of my silence.

Five years have passed since the last letter you sent me, and in that time, I have watched the tides rise and fall, the seasons turn, and the world move on without me. I have not spoken to you, not even in passing, not even in the few letters you sent that were never answered. I have not spoken to you, Eleanor, and I have not known what to say.

But tonight is different. Tonight, the storm is not just a threat to the lighthouse—it is a reckoning. I have spent my life keeping the light burning for others, but I have not kept the light burning for myself. And now, as the waves crash against the cliffs and the wind screams through the stones, I find myself questioning whether I have been a good father, or merely a keeper of the flame who forgot the warmth it was meant to give.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will forgive me.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I know it is unlikely, given the distance between us and the bitterness that has grown in our hearts. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you still remember the sound of my voice or the warmth of our home. Time, as I have learned, is a cruel and unyielding thing, and it has not been kind to us.

Tonight, a storm is gathering on the horizon, and I fear it may not pass without leaving its mark. The winds howl like the spirits of the sea, and the waves crash with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this lighthouse. I have spent my life standing guard over these waters, and though I have seen many storms, I cannot shake the feeling that this one may be different. I do not know if I will make it through the night, and if I do not, I wish for you to know that I have never stopped loving you.

I have always believed in the power of words to mend what time and distance have broken, and so I write this letter with the hope that it may reach you in time. I have no grand words to offer, only the truth of my heart. I have missed you more than I can say, and I regret the pain I have caused you. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1878**

**The Lighthouse, Dunmore Head**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of time that I take up this quill to write to you. I write to you now not only as your father, but as a man who has come to understand the depth of his own failings, and the pain he has caused you.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

You have been a shadow in my life, a presence I have tried to forget, yet one that lingers in the silence of the night. I have not spoken of you to anyone, not even the old sailors who come to visit, for I do not know what words I might say that would not sound like a plea or a reproach.

But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength, I feel the weight of time pressing upon me. I am not the man I was when you left, and I fear that I may not be the man I need to be to face what is to come. I have always believed that the light must shine, no matter the darkness, and I have done so faithfully, though I have never shone for you.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it’s more likely to find you in a state of irritation, as I have a habit of writing at the most inconvenient times. But I’ve waited five long years to put pen to paper, and I feel I must say what’s been weighing on me.

I’ve been keeping the light for nearly thirty years now, and I’ve seen more storms than I care to remember. But tonight, the sky is black as a coal mine, and the wind howls like a banshee. I’ve lit the lamp, and I’ll keep it burning until the last breath leaves me, if need be. It’s a duty I’ve always taken seriously, and I’ve never let the sea take me without a fight.

I know you’ve been angry with me, Eliza. You wrote that letter—yes, I still have it tucked away in my desk. You said I was a fool to leave you and your mother when you were young. I know you think I abandoned you, but I didn’t. I left because I had to. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I’ve spent my life keeping her at bay. You don’t understand what it’s like to be alone in a tower, with only the wind for company and the light to keep the ships from running aground.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it finds me in a state of considerable unrest. The sea has been restless all day, and the wind has been howling like the devil himself has taken up residence in the tower. I write to you now not out of sentiment, but because I fear I may not live to see another dawn.

Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, this life, and this father behind you. I have tried, I truly have tried, to forget the pain of your departure, to ignore the gnawing ache in my chest whenever I think of your name. But the sea has a way of reminding me of things I’d rather not remember, and tonight, it feels as though it's trying to tell me something—something I may not like to hear.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the way you'd run up the stairs with your little boots squeaking, or how you'd insist on climbing the lighthouse tower before you were even old enough to understand the dangers of the sea. You were full of fire, and I was full of patience. I used to think that fire would burn out, that you'd grow up and settle into the quiet life I had planned for you. But you didn't settle, did you? You ran off, left me with the lighthouse and the sea and the silence.

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Quarters**  
**Seagull Point, Isle of Wight**  
**April 3rd, 1892**

*My Dearest Eliza,*

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it’s more likely to find you in a state of disbelief. I’ve written this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee outside these walls, and the sea growling like a beast that’s been roused from its slumber. I can’t tell you how many times I’ve thought of you in the past five years, and how many times I’ve told myself I’d wait until the next calm to write. But tonight, I’m not sure I’ll live to see the dawn.

You remember the last time we spoke, don’t you? That night when the storm broke and the lighthouse nearly collapsed. I was too proud to admit I needed help, and you were too stubborn to stay. I still remember the look in your eyes—betrayal, maybe even pity. I’ve carried that with me ever since.

I’ve been keeping the light burning, as I always have. You know how it is. The sea doesn’t ask for favors, and neither do I. But tonight, it’s different. **My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not be the best time to write. The wind howls like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls, and the sea roars with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I sit by the window, watching the dark clouds churn above, and I feel the weight of years pressing upon me like the tide. It is strange, in this moment of impending chaos, to think of you.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I have not written to you, nor have I called you by your name in these many months. I have told myself that distance is the only way to keep from drowning in the guilt that festers in my heart. But now, as I stand on the precipice of something I may not survive, I find myself wondering if I have been wrong all along.

You were my greatest joy, and my deepest sorrow. I remember the day you left, the way your small hand slipped from mine as you stepped into the carriage, your eyes filled with a mixture of anger and sadness. I told myself it was for the best, that you needed to grow, to find your own path. But I have never stopped wondering what became of you. I have read every letter you sent, every word you wrote, and yet they feel like echoes of a life I no longer know.
